title: QikServe Kiosk
standfirst: Self-service ordering built for high-volume hospitality, designed to feel brand-specific, stay accessible, and hold up under real operational pressure at global scale.

children.text: Context

QikServe builds the self-service ordering layer for hospitality. The kiosk is the most demanding surface in that estate: a single product deployed across thousands of venues, dozens of brands, and dozens of countries, each with its own menu logic, payment setup, and operational habits.

The platform it sits within supports more than 8,000 locations across 42 countries, with over 130 integrations and more than a billion in value handled. The kiosk is not a one-off interface. It is a configurable product that has to behave correctly for a global coffee chain and an independent operator from the same codebase.
Problem

White-label kiosks tend to fail in one of two directions. They either collapse into a generic template that no brand wants to put its name on, or they fragment into bespoke builds that cannot be maintained. Neither scales.

The kiosk also carries an unusually high trust burden. It takes payment, handles allergen and dietary information, verifies age for alcohol, and does it all without a member of staff present. Any ambiguity in the interface becomes an operational problem at the worst possible moment, with a queue building behind the screen.
Constraints

Every decision had to survive contact with real operations: legacy POS integrations, peripheral hardware, intermittent connectivity, accessibility legislation across regions, and brand guidelines that ranged from a loose steer to a strict system.

Accessibility was a hard requirement, not a finishing pass. The kiosk had to meet ADA and WCAG expectations as a physical and digital product at once, which shapes everything from reach and contrast to focus order and error recovery.
Structural model

The answer was to treat the kiosk as a system of configurable behaviours rather than a fixed screen. Menu architecture, modifiers, basket logic, payment, loyalty, tipping, charity donation, age verification, and attract screens were each modelled as components an operator could turn on, restyle, and arrange without touching code.
Brand expression was scoped deliberately. Operators control styling, imagery, and tone within a structure that guarantees the journey still works. The system flexes on surface and holds firm on flow.
Solution

The guest journey was built around clarity under pressure: a strong attract screen, a legible menu structure that copes with deep modifier trees, predictable basket behaviour, and a payment flow that earns trust without staff mediation.

Complex cases were handled as first-class parts of the design rather than exceptions. Coffee customisation, pizza builds, alcohol verification, tipping, and charity prompts each had a defined pattern, so adding them to a deployment did not break the rest of the experience.
Outcomes
The kiosk became the flagship surface of a platform operating across 8,000+ locations and 42 countries, deployed by major global brands alongside independents from the same configurable base.
Accessibility leadership on the kiosk raised the standard across the wider product estate, making participation a design default rather than a retrofit.
Strategic impact

The work showed that white-label does not have to mean generic. A well-structured system let QikServe sell brand-specific experiences at scale without taking on bespoke maintenance, which is a commercial position as much as a design one.

title: QikServe Online Ordering
standfirst: A mature off-premises ordering platform where brand, accessibility, menu complexity and operational reality all shape conversion.

children.text: Context

Online ordering is the web counterpart to the kiosk: a guest-facing ecommerce surface for hospitality brands, covering collection and delivery, configurable menus, payments, loyalty, and location selection. It is where ecommerce behaviour meets hospitality operations.
Problem

Hospitality ecommerce is not generic ecommerce. The catalogue is a menu with modifiers, availability windows, and dietary data. The brand expectations are strict. And the people using it span a far wider range of devices and abilities than a typical retail checkout assumes.
Constraints

The platform had to be mobile-first and meet WCAG 2.1 AA, because accessible participation is both a legal and a commercial requirement. Each operator brought its own menu structure, styling, payment configuration, and fulfilment model, all served from one configurable system.
Solution

The ordering flow was designed around conversion under constraint: a menu structure that handles modifiers and cross-sell without overwhelming the guest, predictable basket behaviour, clear collection and delivery selection, and a payment step that holds trust on a phone.
Item-level and basket-level cross-sell were built as configurable patterns so operators could increase value per order without bolting on friction.
Strategic impact
Treating accessibility and brand as inputs to conversion rather than obstacles to it produced a platform that performs commercially while widening who can actually use it.

title: Strunk
description: A microcopy management product that treats product language as infrastructure, keeping UX writing consistent across an entire platform.

children.text: Context

Strunk started as an internal tool for QikServe and was later rebuilt for Access. Its job is to manage product microcopy: the labels, prompts, errors, and guidance that make an interface usable, across many products and surfaces.
Problem

On a platform with twenty-plus products, language drifts. The same action gets three names, errors contradict each other, and tone fragments. That drift is a real cost: it confuses guests, slows teams, and quietly erodes brand.
Solution
Strunk made product language a managed system rather than scattered strings. Copy could be maintained, governed, and kept consistent in one place, with the structure to scale as the platform grew.
Why it matters

It is a clear piece of systems thinking applied to content. Treating words as infrastructure, with governance and a single source of truth, is the same discipline as a design system, pointed at language.

title: Orson
standfirst: An AI-native venture exploring agent-based workflows, automation and decision support, built independently to turn raw model capability into usable product.

children.text: Context

Orson is an independent venture built outside any employer, exploring what AI looks like as a product rather than a feature. It is proof of building beyond the org chart: applied AI thinking, workflow design, and interface architecture taken from idea to working system.
The bet

Most AI products bolt a chat box onto existing software. Orson starts from the opposite end: what workflows become possible when models can interpret, recommend, and act, and what interface makes that trustworthy to a person.
The work

The focus is the gap between model capability and usable product: agent-based workflows, automation, decision support, and the prompt and interface architecture that turns probabilistic output into something a person can rely on and steer.
Strategic impact

Orson is the founder-energy proof point in the portfolio. It demonstrates AI-native product thinking developed independently, and it connects directly to the platform work: the same systems instinct, now pointed at intelligent software.

title: Richard Murphy
intro: Product design and platform strategy. Design is the method, not the identity.
